name: Court Deadline Calculator

parameters.content:
## Court Deadline Calculator

### How it works

This workflow receives court deadline calculation requests through a webhook, normalizes and validates the payload, and skips processing when required fields are missing. For valid requests, it calculates deadlines using firm configuration stored in n8n Variables, then creates a calendar event in either Google Calendar or Outlook based on the selected platform. It updates the related matter in Clio and records the result in an audit log spreadsheet.

### Setup steps

- Configure the Deadline Webhook URL and ensure callers send the required deadline request fields expected by the normalization code.
- Set the firm details and any deadline-calculation settings in n8n project Variables as referenced by the Calculate Deadlines node.
- Add credentials or authorization headers for Google Calendar and Microsoft Graph/Outlook, depending on which calendar platforms will be used.
- Configure Clio API credentials for creating or updating matter tasks via the Clio endpoint.
- Connect the Google Sheets credential and select the spreadsheet/sheet used for the audit log.

### Customization

Adjust the validation and calculation code to match local court rules, filing types, holidays, and firm-specific reminder policies. The calendar branch can be extended for additional calendar platforms, and the audit log fields can be changed to match reporting needs.

## Receive and validate request

Webhook intake and initial payload preparation. This cluster receives the deadline request, normalizes the incoming data, and checks whether the request has the minimum required fields.

## Handle invalid requests

Lower branch for requests that fail validation. The no-op node stops the invalid path without creating deadlines or external records.

## Calculate and route deadlines

Valid requests continue through deadline computation, using firm details from n8n Variables, then branch according to the selected calendar platform.

## Create calendar events

Parallel calendar output cluster. Depending on the platform decision, the workflow posts the calculated deadline to either Google Calendar or Outlook/Microsoft Graph.

## Update matter and audit

Final recording cluster. After a calendar event is created, the workflow updates the matter in Clio and logs the completed operation to Google Sheets for auditing.

nodes.name: When Deadline Posted
nodes.notes:
Receives the deadline calculation request — from your practice management system, a scheduled cron trigger, or a manual submission form.

Copy the Production URL (visible after you activate the workflow) and paste it into whatever system will trigger deadline calculations for your matters.

Expected payload fields:
  matter_id — your internal matter reference (e.g. 'M-2026-042')
  clio_matter_id — Clio's internal numeric matter ID (find it in the Clio URL: /matters/98765)
  matter_title — the case caption or matter name shown in calendar events
  trigger_date — the date calculations count from, in YYYY-MM-DD (e.g. the complaint filing date, service date, or scheduling order date)
  attorney_email — the attorney's email address (informational; used in calendar event metadata)
  calendar_platform — 'google' or 'outlook' (default: google)
  rules — optional array of deadline rules; if absent, DEFAULT_RULES from Calculate Deadlines are used

The Read Deadline Request step validates the payload and sets is_valid_request = true only when matter_id and a valid trigger_date are present.

nodes.name: Normalize and Validate Payload
nodes.notes:
Reads and validates the incoming webhook payload. Extracts the fields the rest of the workflow needs and sets a flag indicating whether the request is actionable.

Required fields: matter_id (any non-empty string) and trigger_date (YYYY-MM-DD format). Everything else is optional.

If the rules array is absent or empty, the Calculate Deadlines node falls back to its DEFAULT_RULES. This lets you trigger the workflow for every new matter of the same type without passing rules each time — just configure DEFAULT_RULES once in Calculate Deadlines and the same set applies to every trigger.

Output: all normalised fields, plus is_valid_request (true/false), validation_error (message or null), and received_at timestamp.

nodes.name: If Valid Request
nodes.notes:
Decides whether to proceed or stop before any calculation runs.

Yes (matter_id present and trigger_date is a valid date) → proceed to Calculate Deadlines.
No (missing or malformed fields) → route to Skip — invalid request. Check the validation_error field in the Read Deadline Request output to see what was missing.

nodes.name: Ignore Invalid Request
nodes.notes:
Safely ends the workflow for incomplete or malformed requests. No deadlines are calculated, no calendar events are created, and no Clio tasks are written.

This endpoint is visible in the execution history — check the validation_error field in the Read Deadline Request output to see what was missing from the payload.

nodes.name: Compute Court Deadlines
nodes.notes:
Calculates the calendar date of each court deadline, skipping weekends and US federal holidays for business-day rules.

The firm owns the rule set — this workflow never suggests or interprets legal deadlines. Replace the three example placeholder rules with the actual deadline rules your attorney has entered for your practice area and jurisdiction.

Reads from n8n Variables (project → Variables tab):
  FIRM_NAME — your law firm's name
  FIRM_EMAIL — your intake email address
  FIRM_TIMEZONE — your state's timezone (e.g. 'America/Chicago', 'America/Los_Angeles')
  OPS1_SHEET_ID — the ID from your OPS1 Compliance Log sheet URL (same sheet used by the Bar-Compliance Guardrail): .../spreadsheets/d/SHEET_ID/edit

⚙️ Edit DEFAULT_RULES directly in this code node — add your firm's rules; each rule needs:
  name — shown in calendar events and Clio tasks
  offset_days — days from trigger_date (positive = after, negative = before)
  offset_type — 'business' to skip weekends + holidays, 'calendar' to count every day
  description — longer note shown in calendar event body and Clio task description

This node outputs one item per deadline rule. Each downstream node (calendar event, Clio task) runs once per deadline — five rules produce five calendar events and five Clio tasks in a single workflow run.

US federal holidays handled: New Year's Day, MLK Day, Presidents' Day, Memorial Day, Juneteenth, Independence Day, Labor Day, Columbus Day, Veterans Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas. Saturday holidays observe Friday; Sunday holidays observe Monday.

nodes.name: Determine Calendar Platform
nodes.notes:
Routes each deadline to the correct calendar service based on the calendar_platform value in the request.

Google Calendar (true branch) → Create Google Calendar Event
Microsoft Outlook (false branch) → Create Outlook Calendar Event

You only need to wire credentials for the calendar your firm uses. Leave the unused branch's credential blank — that branch never executes.

To default to Outlook, change the condition value from 'google' to 'outlook' and swap the branch labels.

nodes.name: Post to Google Calendar API
nodes.notes:
Creates an all-day calendar event in Google Calendar for each calculated deadline. This node runs once per deadline rule — five rules produce five calendar events.

Credential to wire: In n8n go to project → Credentials tab → New, search for 'Google Calendar OAuth2 API', and connect your Google account. Save it as 'Google Calendar account'.

Calendar: events are created on the attorney's primary Google Calendar. To write to a shared firm calendar instead, replace 'primary' in the URL with the calendar's ID — find it in Google Calendar → Settings → the calendar → Calendar ID (e.g. 'intake@smithlaw.com').

Event title format: 'Rule Name — Matter Title'
Event body: rule description + matter ID + trigger date

If this node fails (e.g. credential not connected), the workflow continues to Update Matter in Clio — deadlines are still written to Clio even if the calendar step does not complete.

nodes.name: Post to Outlook Calendar API
nodes.notes:
Creates a calendar event in Microsoft Outlook via the Microsoft Graph API for each calculated deadline. This node runs once per deadline rule.

Credential to wire: In n8n go to project → Credentials tab → New, search for 'Microsoft Outlook OAuth2 API', and connect your Microsoft 365 account. Save it as 'Outlook account'.

Calendar: events are created in the attorney's primary Outlook calendar at 9:00–9:30 AM on the deadline date. To write to a shared calendar, replace 'me' in the URL with the shared mailbox address: https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/shared@firm.com/events

Timezone: reads from the FIRM_OUTLOOK_TIMEZONE n8n Variable (project → Variables tab). Microsoft Outlook requires Windows timezone format — valid values include 'Eastern Standard Time', 'Central Standard Time', 'Mountain Standard Time', 'Pacific Standard Time'.

If this node fails, the workflow continues to Update Matter in Clio so deadlines are still recorded there.

nodes.name: Update Clio Task API
nodes.notes:
Creates a high-priority task in Clio for each calculated deadline, linked to the matter. This node runs once per deadline rule — five rules produce five Clio tasks in a single run.

Credential to wire:
1. In Clio go to Settings → API Keys → New API key and copy the token
2. In n8n go to project → Credentials tab → New, search for 'Header Auth'
3. Set Name field to 'Authorization' and Value field to 'Bearer YOUR_TOKEN' (include 'Bearer ' with a space)
4. Save it as 'Clio API token'

Clio matter ID: the task is linked to the matter via clio_matter_id in your webhook payload. This must be Clio's internal numeric ID — find it in the URL when viewing the matter in Clio (/matters/98765). If you pass display numbers like 'M-2026-042' instead of the numeric ID, Clio will reject the request. The task is still created; link it to the matter manually inside Clio.

Task properties: name = rule name, due date = calculated deadline date, priority = high, status = pending.

For the full Clio REST API reference see: developer.clio.com

parameters.body: ={{ JSON.stringify({ data: { name: $('Compute Court Deadlines').item.json.rule_name, due_at: $('Compute Court Deadlines').item.json.deadline_date + 'T00:00:00.000Z', description: $('Compute Court Deadlines').item.json.description, matter: { id: $('Compute Court Deadlines').item.json.clio_matter_id }, assignee: { type: 'User' }, status: 'pending', priority: 'high' } }) }}

nodes.name: Append Audit Log in Sheets
nodes.notes:
Appends one audit row per deadline to the Audit Log tab of your OPS1 Compliance Log sheet. Creates a permanent record of every deadline calculated — matter ID, rule name, trigger date, calculated deadline date, and calendar platform used.

Credential to wire: project → Credentials tab → New → search 'Google Sheets OAuth2' → connect with the Google account that owns the OPS1 Compliance Log sheet → save as 'Google Sheets account'.

Sheet ID: set SHEET_ID in the Calculate Deadlines node to the ID from your compliance sheet URL: .../spreadsheets/d/SHEET_ID/edit. This is the same sheet used by the Bar-Compliance Guardrail — use the same ID.

If this step fails (e.g., credentials not wired), the workflow continues — deadlines are still written to Clio and your calendar. A logging failure never blocks deadline creation.

value.message_preview: ={{ ($('Compute Court Deadlines').item.json.rule_name + ' → ' + $('Compute Court Deadlines').item.json.deadline_date + ' (trigger: ' + $('Compute Court Deadlines').item.json.trigger_date + ')').substring(0, 200) }}

rules.name: First Deadline
rules.description: 30 business days from trigger date — firm-entered rule

rules.name: Second Deadline
rules.description: 60 business days from trigger date — firm-entered rule

rules.name: Third Deadline
rules.description: 14 calendar days from trigger date — firm-entered rule

description: Receives a trigger date and rule set via webhook, calculates court deadlines using business-day math (skipping weekends and US federal holidays), creates an all-day calendar event per deadline in Google Calendar or Outlook, and writes each deadline back as a high-priority task in Clio. Replaces DocketCalendar and LawToolBox ($49–$149/user/mo) and guards against the missed-deadline risk that drives 24.6% of malpractice claims. The firm owns and confirms every rule — this workflow never suggests or interprets legal deadlines.
